design_philosophy.core_loop: Navigate single sprawling stage, collect currency (zeny) to upgrade weapons/armor, defeat bosses through mini-stages accessed via green doors, manage limited health with armor system
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Resource scarcity (limited armor hits) vs. exploration reward (hidden weapon pickups under rocks) vs. boss pattern execution
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Commando action hero adapted into futuristic alien invasion scenario; progression from basic flamethrower to arsenal of upgraded weapons
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Deliberately sluggish, near-impossible action design; reuses single-stage format from predecessor but segregates boss encounters into distinct mini-stages. NES version adds economic progression system (zeny purchasing) absent from arcade, creating hybrid run-and-gun/RPG-lite design. Weapon system resets to machine gun on death despite equipment purchases—punitive progression loss.

difficulty.lives_system: Standard lives system; upon losing a life, equipped weapon reverts to machine gun (flamethrower at start) despite prior weapon purchases, creating cumulative penalty
difficulty.checkpointing: Single sprawling stage with no mid-stage checkpoints; green door mini-bosses serve as soft progression gates
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Player can improve odds through zeny-based weapon and armor upgrades, but tight execution demands and weapon reset on death enforce high baseline difficulty. Armor provides temporary hit absorption rather than permanent defense.

progression.upgrade_system: Currency-based (zeny) weapon and armor purchasing. Weapons include flamethrower (starting), machine gun (fallback), bazooka, grenades. Armor reduces hit damage. Weapon pickups scattered throughout stage, often hidden under destructible rocks requiring bazooka or grenade use.
progression.unlock_mechanism: Green door mini-stages gate access to boss encounters; defeating bosses progresses toward main objective. Weapon pickups unlock through exploration and resource expenditure.
replayability_hooks: Hidden weapon locations reward exploration within single-stage design
Zeny collection incentivizes thorough stage traversal for optimal loadouts
Weapon reset on death creates skill-check replay loops

world_building_techniques: Plot premise (time-displaced to future where alien Zang Zip has invaded) provides thematic context but minimal in-game elaboration
Sampled voiceovers from bosses and characters add audio flavor but limited narrative substance
Arcade version features 8-way rotary joystick controls; NES emulates this via fire button depression locking character facing—mechanical rather than narrative design

boss_design.philosophy: Segregated into mini-stages accessed through green doors within main stage; bosses appear to demand pattern recognition and weapon selection strategy
notable_bosses: Zang Zip (primary antagonist from prior game, referenced as current antagonist)

faction_indicators: General Kawasaki (allied authority figure, arranges escape plane)
Paul and Vince (player character identities, commando operatives)
danger_signals: Mysterious storm event displacing protagonists into future timeline
Alien invasion and territorial control by hostile force
Economic scarcity requiring zeny gathering for survival equipment
environmental_storytelling: Single expansive stage implies war-torn or alien-dominated landscape (implied but not detailed)
Destructible rock formations requiring specific weapons suggest environmental puzzle-solving tied to loadout decisions
Mini-stage isolation (green door access) suggests fortified boss positions within larger territory

sources_used: The Cutting Room Floor (Ikari Warriors II: Victory Road NES) - regional differences, developer credit (Micronics), slug/difficulty characterization

Wikipedia Victory Road (video game) - gameplay mechanics (zeny currency, armor system, weapon types, flamethrower→machine gun reset, destructible rocks, mini-stages via green doors), plot summary, control scheme adaptation, arcade predecessor context
